Gstaad

BEBern

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The schoolteacher who dreamed a palace

Robert Steffen, a village schoolteacher in 1905, imagines a grand hotel on the hill above Gstaad. A child helps him convince skeptical farmers that visitors will come if the railway does. Together they watch a vision grow from sketches to a 250-bed palace that will welcome kings and film stars for over a century.

Create This Story

In 1905, village schoolteacher Robert Steffen began purchasing land above Gstaad for a grand hotel. The Gstaad Palace opened on December 8, 1913, with 250 beds in 165 rooms. In 1938, the Scherz family took over management; today Andrea Scherz runs it as one of Europe's last family-run grand hotels. The MOB railway had reached Gstaad on December 20, 1904 — Switzerland's first fully electric railway line — making tourism possible.

The violin that echoed in the church

A child discovers an old violin in the Mauritius Church attic. Playing it summons the ghost of Yehudi Menuhin, who guides the child through the church's 800-year history via music — from the 15th-century frescoes of the Theban Legion to the festival he founded in 1957, proving that great music transforms even the smallest spaces.

Create This Story

The Church of St. Mauritius in Saanen, first documented in 1228, contains remarkable 15th-century frescoes depicting the legend of St. Mauritius and the Theban Legion, rediscovered after being covered during the Reformation. In 1957, violinist Yehudi Menuhin founded a summer music festival, choosing the church for its intimate acoustics. Menuhin received honorary citizenship from Saanen in 1970. Today the festival spans over 50 concerts across seven weeks each summer.

The goat who conquered the world

A proud Saanen goat from the valley learns that her relatives live in over 80 countries. She sets off on an imaginary journey around the world, discovering how far the name "Saanen" has traveled — from a small Swiss valley to becoming the most widely distributed dairy goat breed on earth.

Create This Story

The Saanen goat takes its name from the Saanen valley, bred there for several hundred years. It is a pure white, large dairy goat — the most widely distributed dairy goat breed worldwide, found in over 80 countries, often called the "Holstein of the dairy goat world." Average milk yield is 838 kg per lactation. The breed was first exported internationally in the 19th century.

The bridge between two peaks

A child afraid of heights must cross the Peak Walk suspension bridge at Glacier 3000 — 107 meters long, 80 centimeters wide, the only bridge in the world connecting two mountain summits. At the midpoint, suspended between views of the Matterhorn and Mont Blanc, they discover courage and a spectacular secret hidden in the ice.

Create This Story

The Peak Walk by Tissot, opened in October 2014 at Glacier 3000 near Gstaad, is a 107-meter-long, 80-centimeter-wide suspension bridge connecting two mountain summits — the only one of its kind in the world. From the bridge at 2,971 meters, visitors can see Mont Blanc, the Matterhorn, and the Eiger, Mönch, and Jungfrau. Access is free for Glacier 3000 visitors.

The chalet that wouldn't change

In a village where strict building codes keep every house in traditional wooden style, a rebellious chalet dreams of being made of glass and steel. But when it discovers why its wooden walls, carved balconies, and overhanging roofs were built that way — for warmth, beauty, and belonging — it chooses to stay exactly as it is.

Create This Story

Since the 1950s, Gstaad has enforced strict building regulations requiring all new construction to follow the traditional wooden chalet style — one of the strictest architectural codes in Switzerland. Only timber construction with wooden panelling in the regional style is permitted; modern glass-and-steel designs are banned. Traditional Bernese Oberland chalets feature widely projecting roofs, heavy timber planks, carved balconies, and flower-box facades. These regulations preserve Gstaad's village character despite intense development pressure.

The train that opened the mountains

It is December 1904 and the first MOB electric train arrives in Gstaad. A farm child who has never left the valley takes the train to Montreux and sees Lake Geneva for the first time — discovering that the world beyond the mountains is vast, beautiful, and connected by a ribbon of steel running through the snow.

Create This Story

The Montreux-Oberland Bernois (MOB) railway reached Gstaad on December 20, 1904, and Zweisimmen on July 6, 1905. It was Switzerland's first fully electric railway line. The connection transformed Gstaad from an isolated farming village into an accessible resort. Today the GoldenPass Express panoramic train runs through Gstaad on the Montreux–Interlaken route.

Stars and cowbells

A child in 1960s Gstaad is baffled when famous film stars start appearing in the village. They must help Elizabeth Taylor find her lost cat in a comedy of mistaken identities between celebrities and farmers at the Hotel Olden, where Taylor once said that "film stars and farmers used to sit at the same table."

Create This Story

Elizabeth Taylor bought Chalet Ariel in Gstaad in 1962 and spent winters there with Richard Burton. Julie Andrews and Blake Edwards owned Chalet Fleur de Lys from 1971; Andrews was made honorary citizen of Saanen in 2014. Yehudi Menuhin received honorary citizenship in 1970. Roger Moore, David Niven, and Princess Grace of Monaco were regular visitors. In the 1960s, Time magazine named Gstaad "The Place."

The frescoes that woke up

During a Menuhin Festival concert in the Saanen church, the 15th-century frescoes of St. Mauritius and the Theban Legion come alive. A child must help the painted soldiers find their way back to their wall before the music ends — discovering that art, faith, and music share the same heartbeat.

Create This Story

The Saanen church contains 15th-century frescoes (painted around 1470) depicting the legend of St. Mauritius and the Theban Legion — a Roman legion from Upper Egypt whose soldiers refused to persecute Christians and were martyred. The frescoes were covered during the Reformation and rediscovered in the 20th century. The church serves as the principal venue for the Menuhin Festival, its medieval acoustics lending an incomparable atmosphere to chamber music.
